meta.generator: scripts/export-terms.mjs (re-runnable; do not hand-edit this file)
meta.accumulation_rule: A term renders in the Vocabularium only once earned: its introducing module has at least one completed lesson. status:planned terms belong to unbuilt modules and never render until their module ships. module:"structural" terms are earned with M0 lesson 1 (the game grammar is met immediately).

terms.gloss: The formal unit of inquiry in the Summa. Not a casual question; a structured dispute with objections, a contrary, a body, and replies.
A subdivision of a quaestio. The Summa is organized: Part, Question, Article.
The opening word of every article. Signals that the question is a genuine yes/no with reasons on both sides.
Opens the objections: the case *against* Aquinas's position. Not straw men; these are the strongest opposing reasons.
A single authority (usually Scripture or Aristotle) marshaled against the objections before the body begins.

terms.english: I respond that it must be said
terms.gloss: Opens the body of the article: Aquinas's actual position, argued from principles.

terms.latin: ad primum / ad secundum / ad tertium
terms.gloss: The replies to each objection. Aquinas does not dismiss them; he distinguishes them.
The characteristic scholastic move: not flat contradiction but precision. In one sense, yes; in another sense, no.
The broader academic practice the Summa compresses: public argument, objection and reply.
Citation of a prior author as evidence. In scholastic method, one of the legitimate modes of argument alongside *ratio* (reason).
esse = that a thing is; essentia = what a thing is
terms.latin_tag: *Differt quod est et esse*: "What-it-is and that-it-is are different"

terms.gloss: potentia = capacity, not yet realized; actus = what is right now, realized
terms.latin_tag: *Ex potentia in actum*: "From capacity to actuality"

terms.gloss: res = what is, independent of our thinking about it; distinctio realis = a difference that holds independently of the mind
terms.latin_tag: *In rerum natura*: "In the nature of things"

terms.latin: causa materialis / causa formalis / causa efficiens / causa finalis
terms.english: material cause / formal cause / efficient cause / final cause
terms.gloss: what it is made of / what it is to be / what brought it about / what it is for
materia = the stuff of which a thing is made; forma = the organizing principle that makes it this kind of thing
terms.latin_tag: *Materia prima et forma*: "Prime matter and form"

terms.gloss: substantia = what something is in itself; accidens = a feature that presupposes the thing already exists
Perfection, actuality, that which makes a thing determinate and real. The positive principle. Act is always prior to potency in being.
terms.precision: From Aristotle's *energeia/entelecheia*. In Aquinas, *esse* itself is the supreme act (*actus essendi*). Do not translate as "action"; that is *actio*.

terms.gloss: The real capacity to receive or be limited by an act. Potency is not mere absence; it is a real ontological principle ordered toward act.
terms.precision: Two types exist: active potency (capacity to act *on* another) and passive potency (capacity to receive an act). Module 1 treats primarily passive potency. Never render potency as mere lack; that is the Scotistic error the course corrects.

terms.gloss: God alone. No composition of act and potency; no unrealized capacity, no limitation, no receiving of perfection from another.
terms.precision: The terminus of the argument from motion and from contingency. Every composed being requires an uncomposed cause. The phrase is technical; do not use without the gloss in any lesson where it first appears.

terms.gloss: The union of two really distinct principles in a single being. Every finite being has at least one composition of act and potency.
terms.precision: Three kinds: matter/form (hylomorphic), substance/accident, esse/essentia (metaphysical). The last is the most fundamental. When a lesson uses *compositio* without qualification, it usually means the act/potency composition generally, not any specific kind.

terms.gloss: A positive determination of being. That which constitutes a thing as fully what it is. Act is the source of perfection; potency receives it.
terms.precision: Not "improvement" in the colloquial sense. Perfection here = being what you are, fully. Transcendental perfections (being, truth, goodness) are unlimited in God, limited by essence in creatures.

terms.gloss: The restriction of an act by the potency that receives it. Esse is unlimited in itself; it is limited by the essence that receives it.
terms.precision: Key to the real distinction debate (Module 17). The strict Thomistic line (Cajetan): essence limits esse as a *positive ontological principle*, a real potency to act. The Gilsonian/esseist drift collapses essence to a mere shadow or mode of esse. This tension is not resolved until Module 17.

terms.gloss: Capacity to act on another: to produce, to move, to cause.
terms.precision: Distinct from passive potency. Fire has active potency with respect to wood; wood has passive potency with respect to fire. Both are real.

terms.gloss: Capacity to receive an act from another.
terms.precision: This is the primary sense in the act/potency pair as introduced. Passive does not mean defective; it is the ontological condition for receiving any perfection.

terms.gloss: That by which a thing is. The innermost act of all acts and the perfection of all perfections (*actus omnium actuum, perfectio omnium perfectionum*). Not existence as a bare fact but the very actuality of a being.
terms.precision: Gilson's existential emphasis is correct: *esse* is not just logical existence; it is the most intense metaphysical principle. But *esse* must be received by a real essence; it is not self-diffusive to the point of swallowing essences (contra the Gilsonian drift). Do not translate as simply "existence"; that loses the act-character.

terms.gloss: Aquinas's technical term for *esse* considered as act. The act by which a being is. Distinct from the essence that receives it.
terms.precision: The term that makes Aquinas's metaphysics decisively different from Aristotle's: not just form as act, but *esse* as the act of the form itself. When teaching this, always pair with *essentia*; the two are inseparable in the exposition even though really distinct in creatures.

terms.gloss: That which a thing is: the quiddity, the whatness, the nature. In creatures, essence is a real ontological principle distinct from esse and ordered toward it as potency to act.
terms.precision: Not mere concept, not mere limitation. The strict Thomistic line (Cajetan, Garrigou-Lagrange): essence is a *positive* ontological principle, a real potency to receive esse. The Gilsonian drift makes essence a shadow or limitation of esse, which (on the strict reading) dissolves it. This is the fault line of Module 17.

terms.gloss: Literally "what-ness." Synonym for essence, emphasizing the answer to "what is it?" (*quid est?*).
terms.precision: Interchangeable with *essentia* in most contexts. *Quidditas* tends to appear when the emphasis is epistemological (what the intellect grasps); *essentia* tends to appear in ontological contexts (the real principle).

terms.gloss: Essence considered as the principle of operation. What a thing is, considered as the source of its characteristic activities.
terms.precision: Three distinct uses in the curriculum: (1) here, as synonym for essence-as-operative-principle; (2) in Module 4, as the nature that a *suppositum* possesses and operates through; (3) colloquially (avoid). Always gloss on first use in each module.

terms.gloss: A distinction that holds in the thing itself, independently of any mind considering it. Esse and essentia are really distinct in every creature.
terms.precision: The "really distinct" claim is what separates Aquinas from all mere conceptual-distinction readings. The Gilsonian camp accepts the language but (on the strict reading) empties it: if essence is just esse's own self-limitation, the distinction is not between two positive principles. This is M17 content; introduce here as the claim, develop in M17 as the debate.

terms.gloss: Being as it is predicated of all beings: the most general notion of being. Distinct from *esse* as the individual act of a particular being.
terms.precision: The universal notion under which all beings fall, but which has no existence apart from individual beings. Avoid introducing without glossing both senses of *esse*; this is a place where the shift between levels confuses beginners.

terms.gloss: A being: the thing that has *esse*. From the present participle of *esse*.
terms.precision: Distinguished from *esse* as the act: *ens* is the thing that is; *esse* is the act by which it is. In transcendental contexts (Module 6), *ens* is the first transcendental.

terms.gloss: The relation by which a finite being *has* esse without *being* esse. Every creature participates in being; God alone *is* being (*esse subsistens*).
terms.precision: Platonic structure deployed in Thomistic metaphysics: the many participate in the one not by emanation but by real causation (efficient and exemplary). Participation without a *real* essence to do the participating collapses into Neoplatonism.

terms.gloss: Being that subsists through itself, by its own essence. God alone. Every other being has esse *by reception*.
terms.precision: The contrast term for created being. God is *esse subsistens*; creatures *have* esse. The distinction between having and being esse is the metaphysical root of the Creator/creature divide.

terms.gloss: Pure potency. Matter stripped of every determination: no size, no color, no properties. The ultimate subject of substantial change.
terms.precision: Never exists without form; it is a limiting concept, not an empirical residue. "Show me prime matter" is the wrong question; the right question is what is left when you subtract every formal determination conceptually. Do not conflate with "the raw materials" of a thing; that is *materia secunda* (already determined matter).

terms.gloss: The act by which a thing *is* what it is at the most fundamental level: not a feature it has, but what makes it this *kind* of thing.
terms.precision: Not the shape (that is a spatial accident). The substantial form of a human being is the soul. When substantial form is lost, the thing does not merely change; it ceases to be that kind of thing. This is why death is not change but corruption.

terms.gloss: A form that modifies an already-existing substance without constituting it. Color, size, disposition: all accidental forms.
terms.precision: Note the overlap with *accidens* from M0: accidental forms are what give rise to accidents. The distinction form/accident is a way of speaking about the same reality from different directions.

terms.gloss: The whole substance resulting from the union of matter and form. A concrete individual being.
terms.precision: Not a mere sum of two things; matter and form are principles *of* the composite, not separable parts. The composite is the actual existing thing; matter and form are real but not separately existing.

terms.gloss: Change in which a substance comes to be: a new substantial form is acquired and the old one lost.
terms.precision: Requires *materia prima* as the continuous subject: something must persist through the change (matter), even as the form is entirely replaced. Contrast with *alteratio*.

terms.gloss: Change in which a substance ceases to be: its substantial form is lost and another acquired by the matter. The paper's side of the burning.
terms.precision: One event, two descriptions: the corruption of one substance *is* the generation of another (*generatio unius est corruptio alterius*). Never a gap of bare matter in between. Death is the paradigm corruption of a living thing, not its largest alteration.

terms.gloss: Change in which a substance acquires or loses an accidental form while remaining the same substance.
terms.precision: Socrates tanning, water warming, iron rusting: the substance persists, an accident changes. The contrast with *generatio* is crucial: substantial form lost means the thing is gone; accidental form lost means the thing merely changed.

terms.gloss: The principle by which one member of a species is this individual rather than that one. Socrates is individuated from Plato by designated matter: this particular matter, here and now.
terms.precision: Designated (*signata*) matter = matter as actually located and quantified. The key point: what makes Socrates Socrates is not his form (that is shared with other humans) but the particular matter this form informs. This creates the classic problem of angels: immaterial, they have no matter to individuate them, so each angel is its own species.

terms.gloss: The concrete individual of a nature: the particular this-thing, considered as existing, subsisting, and acting in its own right.
terms.precision: Every *suppositum* has a nature; not every nature is a *suppositum* (hypostatic union). The *suppositum* is what answers "who?" or "what individual?" and not "what kind?" (that is the nature/essence).

terms.gloss: A *suppositum* of a rational nature. A rational individual subsisting in its own right.
terms.precision: Boethius's definition: *naturae rationalis individua substantia* (individual substance of a rational nature). The key Christological application: Christ has two natures (divine and human) but *one person* (one divine *suppositum*). The human nature does not have its own *suppositum*; it subsists in the divine person.

terms.gloss: Greek synonym for *suppositum*. Used in Trinitarian and Christological theology: one divine nature, three hypostases (persons).
terms.precision: Technical term from the Greek councils. In Latin scholasticism, interchangeable with *suppositum* in most contexts. The Christological controversy (*hypostatic union*) is the paradigm case.

terms.gloss: The mode by which a nature exists in its own right: as its own subject, not as a modification of another.
terms.precision: What is added to a nature to make it a *suppositum*. The debate: is *subsistentia* something real added to the nature, or just the nature itself considered as not inhering in another? Thomism tends toward the former.

terms.gloss: The broader class under which a species falls. Predicated of many species with respect to what they are.
terms.precision: "Animal" is the genus of "human being." The genus is predicated essentially but not specifically; it does not capture what makes a species *this* species.

terms.gloss: The specific kind. Predicated of individuals essentially and specifically.
terms.precision: "Human being" is the species under the genus "animal." Do not conflate with the biological taxonomy sense; here *species* means the precise kind, fully characterized.

terms.gloss: The feature that distinguishes one species from another within the same genus.
terms.precision: "Rational" is the differentia of "human being" within "animal." Genus + differentia = definition.

terms.gloss: A feature that follows necessarily from the essence even though it is not part of the definition.
terms.precision: "Capable of laughter" (*risibile*) is a *proprium* of human beings; it follows from rationality, but rationality is not the same as the capacity for laughter. *Proprium* is convertible with the species but not identical to the essence.

terms.gloss: A feature that a subject can have or lack without ceasing to be what it is.
terms.precision: This is *accidens* in the predicable sense, not the category sense. Being pale, being tall: these are accidents of a human being that the human being can lose. Distinct from accidental form (ontological) and from *accidens* as the 2nd-10th categories. Register all three uses separately.

terms.gloss: Being that exists in itself. The first and primary category; the others are modes of accident.
The extension of matter; makes a substance large or small, divisible into parts.
A modification of substance that specifies it further: color, shape, virtue, skill, disposition.
The ordering of one thing to another: fatherhood, similarity, equality.
The influx of a cause on an effect; the exercise of active power.
The receiving of an action: being moved, being heated, being known.
The presence of a body in a place.
Position in time.
The ordering of parts in place: sitting, standing, prone.
The relation of a body to what surrounds or covers it: being clothed, armed.

terms.english: per se in the first mode
terms.gloss: The predicate is in the definition of the subject. "Animal is sensitive": sensitivity is part of what it is to be an animal.

terms.english: per se in the second mode
terms.gloss: The subject is in the definition of the predicate. "The snub is in the nose": snubness requires nose to be defined.

terms.english: per se in the third mode
terms.gloss: Predication of substance. "Socrates is a man": this is said of Socrates not as of a subject, but as of what Socrates *is*.

terms.english: per se in the fourth mode
terms.gloss: Cause-and-effect: the predicate follows from the subject by necessity. "The triangle has 180 degrees."
Predication that does not belong through the subject's nature; it just happens to be the case.
A formal argument in which a conclusion follows necessarily from two premises sharing a middle term.
terms.precision: The tool, not the goal. Aquinas values demonstration over bare syllogism; a valid syllogism from false premises proves nothing.

terms.gloss: A syllogism that produces *scientia* (certain knowledge) because its premises are necessary and per se.
terms.precision: The strict sense requires *per se primo modo* or *per se quarto modo* predication in at least one premise. Most argument in philosophy is dialectic, not demonstration.

terms.gloss: Demonstration from cause to effect: the "why." The highest form; explains not only that something is the case but *why*.
terms.precision: From *propter quid* = "on account of which." This is the gold standard of scientia.

terms.gloss: Demonstration from effect to cause: the "that." Valid but less perfect; proves something is the case without explaining why.
terms.precision: From *quia* = "that." The arguments for God's existence are largely *demonstratio quia* (from effects to the cause we cannot directly observe).

terms.gloss: Argument from probable premises; the form most philosophical discussion actually takes.
terms.precision: Not inferior to demonstration in all contexts; dialectic is the mode of genuine inquiry when demonstration is unavailable. Do not treat as a slur.

terms.gloss: Movement from particular instances to a universal principle.
terms.precision: In Aristotle's and Aquinas's epistemology, induction is what grounds the first principles of demonstration; it is not mere generalization but an intellectual grasp of the universal in the particular.

terms.gloss: Knowledge through causes: certain, universal, and necessary. The goal of demonstration.
terms.precision: Not "science" in the modern empirical sense. *Scientia* in scholastic philosophy = systematic knowledge ordered by causes. Theology is a *scientia* (subalternated to the science of the blessed).

terms.gloss: The first transcendental. Everything that is, insofar as it is.
terms.precision: Not "beings" (plural) but *being* as a noun: the participial form of *esse*. Already introduced in Module 2 but now systematized.

terms.gloss: What a being is: the *quidditas* side of being. Being considered as having a nature.
terms.precision: Note: *res* was introduced at M0 in the *distinctio realis* lesson (real thing, independent of mind). Here it enters the transcendental list as a positive characterization.

terms.gloss: Unity: every being is undivided in itself.
terms.precision: Not numerical oneness but transcendental unity; every being is one thing, not internally contradictory.

terms.gloss: Every being is knowable in principle; capable of being known by an intellect.
terms.precision: Transcendental truth is in the *intellect* (the knowing mind) as conforming to being. *Adaequatio rei et intellectus* works in both directions: the thing is true when it conforms to a creative intellect (divine mind); the intellect is true when it conforms to the thing.

terms.gloss: Every being is desirable in principle; ordered toward an end.
terms.precision: Transcendental goodness = ontological perfection plus orderedness to an end. The good does not add a feature to being; it expresses being from the perspective of appetite and will.

terms.gloss: Every being, insofar as it has integrity, proportion, and clarity, is beautiful.
terms.precision: Debated: is *pulchrum* a distinct transcendental or reducible to *verum* plus *bonum*? Aquinas treats it as connected to both (it pleases when seen). The course should flag this as a genuine open question in the tradition.

terms.gloss: Every being is *other* than what it is not.
terms.precision: The negative transcendental: being is *aliquid* = *aliud quid* = "other than something." Not nothing, not its negation.

terms.gloss: The transcendentals are coextensive with being; they add no new beings, only a new perspective.
terms.precision: The scholastic axiom. Misunderstanding this produces the error of thinking the transcendentals are a list of *types* of being rather than aspects of *all* being.

terms.gloss: The matter out of which something is made: the underlying subject of change.
The form that makes a thing *this kind* of thing: the organizing and specifying principle.
The agent that brings a thing into being: the source of motion or change.
The end or goal for the sake of which the agent acts.
The terminus toward which an agent acts. Every agent acts for an end; this is the principle of finality.
The end built into the action itself by its nature: what this type of action is ordered to.
The additional intention the agent brings to the action.
A cause that acts only in virtue of being moved by a principal cause. The carpenter's tool.
The substantial form of a living body. Not a thing inside the body; the form that makes the body *this kind* of body.
terms.precision: Hylomorphic definition, not Cartesian dualism. The soul is not imprisoned in the body; it is the body's formal principle. The rational soul is the form of the human body.

terms.gloss: The soul-level responsible for nutrition, growth, and reproduction. Present in plants, animals, and humans.
terms.precision: Each level includes the powers of the levels below. The rational soul includes vegetative and sensitive powers.

terms.gloss: The soul-level responsible for sensation and local motion. Present in animals and humans.
terms.precision: Includes both interior and exterior senses, plus appetite.

terms.gloss: The soul-level responsible for intellect and rational will. Proper to humans.
terms.precision: The human soul is the only material form that is also subsistent; it can exist without the body (demonstrating immortality). This is the key difference from animal sensitive souls.

terms.gloss: The faculties through which the soul operates: its distinct operative capacities.
terms.precision: The powers are distinguished by their acts, and acts by their objects. A real distinction holds between the soul and its powers in Aquinas (disputed by some).

terms.gloss: The five external senses: sight, hearing, smell, taste, touch.
terms.precision: Each faculty is specified by its proper sensible: color for sight, sound for hearing, etc.

terms.gloss: Common sense, imagination, cogitative power (*vis cogitativa* in humans, *vis aestimativa* in animals), and memory.
terms.precision: The interior senses process and integrate what the exterior senses deliver. The *phantasma* is their product.

terms.gloss: The representation of a sensible object retained and worked on by the interior senses. The material from which the active intellect abstracts the intelligible species.
terms.precision: Not a mental picture in the Cartesian sense. The *phantasma* is the sense-level representation; the *species intelligibilis* is the intellect's own reception of the form.

terms.gloss: The rational power of the soul ordered to the universal and the immaterial.
terms.precision: Distinguished into active (*intellectus agens*) and possible (*intellectus possibilis*). Not identical with *ratio* (discursive reasoning), which is the movement from one known thing to another.

terms.gloss: The abstractive power: what strips the *phantasma* of its individuating material conditions and produces the *species intelligibilis*.
terms.precision: Does the active work of making the intelligible form actually intelligible. Aquinas holds it is part of the soul (against Averroes's separate intellect).

terms.gloss: The receptive power: what receives the *species intelligibilis* and is thereby actualized toward understanding.
terms.precision: "Possible" = potential, receptive. Before receiving the species, it is like a blank tablet. Once actualized, it forms the *verbum mentis*.

terms.gloss: The rational appetite: the appetite proper to a being with intellect, ordered to the good as known.
terms.precision: Not identical with desire (*appetitus sensitivus*). The will is ordered to the good *as intellectually apprehended*, not just felt.

terms.gloss: The faculty of self-determination with respect to means. The will freely chooses among goods that do not compel it.
terms.precision: Not freedom from the good; the will is necessarily ordered to the good in general. Freedom operates with respect to particular goods that do not exhaust the good.

terms.gloss: The operation by which the active intellect extracts the universal intelligible form from the particular phantasm.

The three levels at which the intellect abstracts from matter: (1) natural philosophy, from individual but not common matter; (2) mathematics, from all sensible matter; (3) metaphysics, from all matter.
The form by which the intellect knows an object: not the object itself, but the likeness through which the intellect is assimilated to the object.
The concept formed by the intellect after it has been actualized by the species: the product of understanding.
Simple apprehension (grasping a quiddity), judgment (composing or dividing), reasoning (moving from known to unknown).
The scholastic definition of truth: the mind's conformity to the thing, or (in divine understanding) the thing's conformity to the mind.

A form predicated of many: genus, species, differentia, proprium, accident. Aquinas's position: universals exist formally in the mind but have a foundation in things (*universale post rem* as mental, *universale in re* as foundation).
Whatever represents something other than itself to a knowing faculty.
A sign that represents without first being known itself: the *species intelligibilis* is the paradigm. It is the medium through which the object is known, not an object of knowledge in its own right.
A sign that is first known as a thing and then as representing something else: words, images, smoke as sign of fire.
A sign that signifies by nature, not by convention: smoke signifies fire naturally.
A sign that signifies by convention: words in any natural language.
The relation of sign to signified: the formal character of a sign as sign.
The grammatical-logical form in which a term signifies its object; may differ from the *modus essendi* (way of being) of the object. Key for the theology of divine names.
The object to which the sign refers.
The reference of a term as used in a particular context: what the term stands for in a given utterance.
The interior representation formed by the intellect: the formal sign par excellence.
A term that signifies a thing with a determinate mode: the primary bearer of *significatio* in language.
The faculty ordered to the good: the tendency of a being toward what perfects it.
The part of the sensitive appetite ordered to the pleasurable good: love, desire, delight, hatred, aversion, grief.
The part of the sensitive appetite ordered to the difficult good: hope, despair, courage, fear, anger.
The movements of the sensitive appetite: not mere feelings but real ontological motions toward or away from apprehended goods and evils.
The first and most fundamental passion: the affective response to the apprehended good. All other passions are reducible to love and its derivatives.
An act elicited with knowledge and will: an act for which the agent is morally responsible.
terms.precision: Distinct from *actus hominis*. This distinction is one of the most important QA items in M12; every exercise touching morality must use these correctly.

terms.gloss: An act that happens in a human being but without full deliberation or freedom: reflexes, digestion, breathing, acts of passion exceeding rational control.
terms.precision: Not morally imputable in the full sense. The *actus humanus* / *actus hominis* cut is prior to all discussion of moral evaluation.

terms.gloss: The three elements that together determine the moral character of an act: object, end, and circumstances. All three must be good for the act to be good.
terms.precision: A common error is treating intention (*finis operantis*) as the sole or dominant determinant. The object (*finis operis*) specifies the act first. An act intrinsically evil by object cannot be made good by intention.

terms.gloss: The proximate end of the action as defined by reason: what the action *is*, prescinding from circumstances and the agent's further intention.
terms.precision: Not the physical act but the act as specified by reason. "Killing an innocent person" and "executing a criminal" may be physically similar but are different moral objects.

terms.gloss: The morally relevant conditions surrounding an act: who, what, where, by what means, why, how, when.
terms.precision: Circumstances can increase or diminish moral goodness or evil; they cannot change a bad act into a good one. Common error: thinking circumstances determine morality in full (consequentialism).

terms.gloss: The agent's further purpose in performing the act: the *finis operantis*.
terms.precision: A good intention does not sanctify an evil act. "The end does not justify the means" = the *finis operantis* does not override a bad *objectum*.

terms.gloss: The final end of the human person: perfect fulfillment of the rational appetite in the vision of God.
terms.precision: Not mere subjective contentment. *Beatitudo* is objective: the intellect's perfect act directed to perfect truth (*Deus sub ratione bonitatis*). Natural happiness (the philosopher's) is real but insufficient; supernatural beatitude requires grace.

terms.gloss: The last end: that for the sake of which all other ends are pursued, which is itself pursued for its own sake and not as a means.
terms.precision: Everything the will does is ordered (explicitly or implicitly) to *finis ultimus*. Aquinas argues this must be one; the will cannot have two ultimate ends.

terms.gloss: The structure of practical reasoning: universal principle, particular application, action.
terms.precision: Not the same as theoretical syllogism; the conclusion is an *action*, not a proposition. *Synderesis* supplies the major premise; *conscientia* applies it to the case.

terms.gloss: The habitual knowledge of the first principles of practical reason: the innate inclination toward the good and away from evil.
terms.precision: Not a faculty but a *habitus*: a stable disposition of the practical intellect. The major premise of the practical syllogism is supplied by *synderesis*.

terms.gloss: The act of applying moral knowledge to a particular act: practical reason in its application to concrete choice.
terms.precision: Not the *synderesis* (which holds the principles); conscience is the *application*. Error in conscience is possible and sometimes excusable; a *certain* conscience (even if objectively erroneous) must be followed.

terms.gloss: The foundational principles of natural law, self-evident to practical reason: "Do good and avoid evil" as the primary; specific inclinations beneath it.
terms.precision: The foundational principle is formal; it does not specify what counts as good. The content comes from the natural inclinations.

terms.gloss: The rational ordering of all things by God: the divine reason directing all things to their proper ends. The ultimate foundation of all law.
terms.precision: Not arbitrary divine will (voluntarism) but divine *reason*. The eternal law is not promulgated to humans directly; it is participated through natural law.

terms.gloss: The rational creature's participation in the eternal law. The natural inclinations of reason, properly ordered, toward the good.
terms.precision: Not a list of rules but a *participation* in the eternal law: the creature's innate rational ordering toward its proper good and end. The content is derived from the natural inclinations (life, procreation, truth, society).

terms.gloss: Particular determinations of natural law made by legitimate authority for the common good.
terms.precision: *Lex humana* is just only when it conforms to *lex naturalis*. An unjust law is not law in the full sense (*lex iniusta non est lex*: Augustine, cited by Aquinas).

terms.gloss: Law directly promulgated by God through revelation: Old Law and New Law.
terms.precision: Supplements natural law for what reason alone cannot determine (sacramental form, specific ritual prescriptions) or where natural law is unclear in fallen human reason.

terms.gloss: The act of the will by which it freely selects a particular good among alternatives presented by reason.
terms.precision: *Electio* presupposes *consilium* (deliberation) and *intentio* (intention of the end). It is the joint act of will and reason most properly characteristic of moral life.

terms.gloss: A stable disposition of a faculty that inclines it readily and pleasantly toward a certain kind of act.
terms.precision: Note: *habitus* as category (Module 5) = "having" as accidental category. *Habitus* here = virtue-related disposition. Same word, different sense. Always gloss in context. The virtue *habitus* is a quality (second category), not the state-of-having category.

terms.gloss: A good habitus: a stable disposition that inclines toward acts perfective of the agent.
terms.precision: From *vir* (man) with the sense of force. Not mere behavior pattern; virtue is a real quality of the soul that makes the agent capable of acting well readily, pleasantly, and in the right way.

terms.gloss: Faith, hope, and charity: virtues infused by God, ordered immediately to God as their object.
terms.precision: Not acquired by human effort but infused. They exceed the natural order and cannot be obtained by repetition of acts.

terms.gloss: Prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance: the four "hinge" virtues on which the moral life turns.
terms.precision: "Cardinal" from *cardo* (hinge). Acquired by habituation; each has an intellectual and volitional dimension. Prudence governs the others.

terms.gloss: The intellectual virtue that governs practical reason: right reason applied to action (*recta ratio agibilium*).
terms.precision: Not mere shrewdness or calculation. Prudence is the virtue that enables the agent to find the right *means* to the right *end* in the particular case. It presupposes right ends (virtue of will) and delivers right choice.

terms.gloss: The five virtues of the theoretical and practical intellect: understanding (*intellectus*), science (*scientia*), wisdom (*sapientia*), art (*ars*), prudence (*prudentia*).
terms.precision: Acquired (except perhaps for the light of *intellectus*). They perfect the intellect as intellect, not as orderer of action (except prudence).

terms.gloss: The intellectual virtue that considers the highest causes: the supreme perfection of the theoretical intellect.
terms.precision: Wisdom judges all things by the highest causes and orders the other intellectual virtues under itself. In theology, sacra doctrina is a wisdom (*sapientia*) because it judges all things by divine revelation.

terms.gloss: A distinction holding between two things that actually differ in the thing itself, independently of any mind.
A distinction that holds only in the mind's consideration of one thing under different aspects.

terms.english: distinction of reason with a foundation in reality
terms.gloss: A mental distinction that is not arbitrary; it has a real foundation in the thing's different relations or aspects.
A mental distinction with no foundation in the thing; purely a product of the mind's double consideration.
Scotus's third option between real and mental: a distinction in the thing itself but short of a real distinction between two really separable items.
A distinction between a thing and its mode: less than a real distinction, more than a mental one.
In itself / through another. Per se = through the thing's own nature; per accidens = through something else happening to be the case.
Substance (*in se*) vs. accident (*in alio*): the fundamental ontological divide.
First act = the form or soul constituting a being; second act = the operation flowing from that form. Soul is first act of the body; seeing is second act of the eye.
A term predicated of multiple subjects in exactly the same sense: "animal" said of dogs and cats.
A term predicated of multiple subjects in entirely different senses: "bat" (flying mammal vs. baseball implement).
A term predicated of multiple subjects in a sense that is partly the same, partly different: "healthy" said of the organism, the food, the complexion, the medicine.

Analogy in which one subject is the primary analogate and others are secondary, related to the primary. "Healthy" primarily of the organism; secondarily of food (causing health), urine (signifying it), etc.

Analogy in which the same relation holds in each analogate: as A is to B, so C is to D. Being said of substance and accident: as substance is to its being, so accident is to its being, but differently.
The method of knowing God by denying finite perfections of him: God is not limited, not composite, not temporal.
The method of knowing God by affirming finite perfections of him in an unlimited, eminent mode: God is not merely good but *goodness itself*.
See Structural Terms above. Here treated as a formal object of study, not just a UI label.
See Structural Terms above.
See Structural Terms above.

terms.english: I respond that it must be said
terms.gloss: See Structural Terms above.
See Structural Terms above.
The three-part structure: Prima Pars (God and creation), Secunda Pars (the moral order, divided Prima Secundae and Secunda Secundae), Tertia Pars (Christ and the sacraments).
Aquinas's description of *esse*: the most fundamental actuality, on which all other acts depend.
*Esse* as the supreme perfection: all other perfections are participations in or specifications of it.
The strict Thomistic reading: *esse* is an act, and it is really distinct from the essence that receives and limits it.
The strict Thomistic position: essence is the potency that receives and limits *esse*. Essence is *positive*, a real ontological principle, not a shadow.
